title: System Design Test
description: Test your system-design knowledge with 20 questions

questions.question: What is the recommended approach for implementing a rate limiting system for a financial trading platform that needs to adjust limits based on market volatility?

options.text: Use a static token bucket with fixed limits that remain constant regardless of market conditions
Implement a dynamic rate limiter using market data feeds with a multi-tier token bucket system and circuit breaker patterns
Apply fixed per-minute limits regardless of market volatility since trading platforms require consistent rules
Use a simple sliding window rate limiter with no adjustment capability based on market conditions

questions.explanation: A dynamic rate limiter uses market data feeds to detect volatility levels and automatically adjusts limits through a multi-tier token bucket system with circuit breakers for protection during extreme conditions.

questions.question: What CI/CD approach would you use for a microservices application with 10 services in separate repositories?

options.text: GitOps-based CI/CD pipeline using ArgoCD or Flux for declarative deployment management across all services
Traditional CI/CD with manual deployment scripts triggered by commits to each service repository
Single monolithic pipeline that builds and deploys all services together from a central repository
Manual deployment process where operators deploy each service independently after code review

questions.explanation: GitOps with ArgoCD or Flux enables declarative deployment management across all 10 services with automated synchronization from git repositories.

questions.question: What is the recommended architecture for a notification service that sends alerts via email, SMS, and push notifications from event streams?

options.text: Synchronous HTTP calls from upstream services directly to notification providers
Use an asynchronous message queue such as Kafka or RabbitMQ to decouple services, with publish-subscribe model for user channel subscriptions
Direct database polling where the notification service periodically checks for new events
WebSocket connections maintained by each upstream service to push notifications in real-time

questions.explanation: An asynchronous message queue decouples upstream services from notification delivery, and publish-subscribe allows users to subscribe to specific notification channels.

questions.question: How would you design a global load-balancing layer for a real-time collaboration platform supporting WebSocket presence and per-tenant quotas?

options.text: Single load balancer in one region handling all global traffic
Use a geo-aware HAProxy tier per region with nginx terminating HTTP/WebSocket, global Anycast DNS in front, with runtime reconfiguration and per-tenant quotas
Round-robin DNS load balancing without WebSocket awareness or regional optimization
Single-region load balancer with global CDN for static content only

questions.explanation: Geo-aware HAProxy with regional deployment, nginx for protocol termination, and Anycast DNS provides low-latency routing while maintaining presence and quota enforcement.

questions.question: How do you ensure a specific pod only runs on nodes with SSD storage in a 3-node Kubernetes cluster?

options.text: Use Pod Disruption Budgets to force pod rescheduling
Use Node Affinity for soft/hard preferences based on node labels, or Taints/Tolerations to repel pods from certain nodes
Manually specify node selector in pod spec without using any K8s scheduling features
Use Resource quotas to limit which pods can be scheduled

questions.explanation: Node Affinity selects FOR nodes with specific labels, while Taints/Tolerations repel pods from nodes - affinity is preferred for positive selection.

questions.question: What rate limiting approach would you use for a CDN that needs different limits based on content type?

options.text: Single global rate limit applied uniformly to all content
Implement a multi-tier rate limiting system using Redis with geo-based sharding, combining global token buckets for static assets and regional sliding windows for dynamic APIs
Per-request rate limiting without geo-distribution or content-type differentiation
Rate limit only at origin servers without edge caching considerations

questions.explanation: A multi-tier system with geo-sharded Redis uses token buckets for static content and sliding windows for dynamic APIs, with consistent hashing for distribution.

questions.question: How would you design a distributed caching strategy to prevent cache stampede when a popular product's cache expires simultaneously?

options.text: Immediately refresh all expired cache entries in parallel by all concurrent requests
Use probabilistic early expiration, request coalescing with mutex/lock mechanism, and background refresh to prevent thundering herd
Let all requests hit the database simultaneously and let the database handle the load
Use a single long TTL so entries rarely expire together

questions.explanation: Probabilistic early expiration staggers expiry times, request coalescing prevents duplicate DB queries, and background refresh keeps cache warm.

questions.question: What is the recommended approach for designing a unified API gateway for a financial platform exposing REST, gRPC, and GraphQL?

options.text: Maintain three separate API endpoints without a unified gateway layer
Implement a single edge gateway with protocol adapters for REST, gRPC, and GraphQL, with a shared in-memory router mapping routes to backend services
Route all traffic through separate load balancers for each protocol without gateway translation
Convert all requests to REST before sending to internal microservices

questions.explanation: A unified gateway with protocol adapters handles all three transports while maintaining a shared router to backend services like OrderService and MarketDataService.

questions.question: How would you design a lightweight API gateway for a multi-tenant SaaS backend that exposes REST, gRPC, and GraphQL?

options.text: Build three separate entry points without gateway consolidation
Build a gateway service where REST routes map to internal gRPC methods via generated stubs, GraphQL resolvers fetch from multiple services
Use a simple HTTP proxy that only handles REST without protocol transformation
Require clients to convert their protocols before making requests

questions.explanation: The gateway translates REST to gRPC via stubs, resolves GraphQL from multiple services, and authenticates per-tenant through a single entry point.

questions.question: What is the recommended pattern for exposing a Notes API in REST, GraphQL, and gRPC?

options.text: Create three separate implementations for each protocol without any shared logic
Model Note with id, authorId, content, updatedAt; expose via REST at /v1/notes, GraphQL with Query/Mutation, and gRPC NoteService using a gateway to translate
Only support REST and require other protocols to be converted by clients
Use separate databases for each protocol to ensure isolation

questions.explanation: A unified gateway translates between transports while using a single data model across all protocols.

questions.question: How would you design a health check system for a load balancer that detects server degradation before complaints occur?

options.text: Use passive health checks only - only detect failures after requests fail
Implement proactive health checks with multiple metrics like response time and error rates, dynamically weighting traffic away from degrading servers
Ping servers every 60 seconds and remove them only when completely unresponsive
Rely on user complaints to identify unhealthy servers

questions.explanation: Proactive health checks monitor multiple metrics and use this data to dynamically weight traffic away from degrading servers before failures occur.

questions.question: What caching strategy would you use for a ride-sharing platform handling 100,000 concurrent drivers with location updates?

options.text: Use a single central database with no caching for driver locations
Implement a tiered caching strategy with Redis Cluster for geo-replicated driver location data and write-behind pattern for batched updates
Cache all driver data in each region without any replication strategy
Store driver locations in a database without any in-memory caching layer

questions.explanation: Redis Cluster with geo-replication and consistent hashing provides low-latency access to driver locations with write-behind batching.

questions.question: How would you design a distributed caching system for a real-time analytics platform processing streaming event data?

options.text: Use a simple single-level cache with long TTLs for all data
Implement a multi-tier caching approach with write-through cache, time-series partitioning, and TTL-based eviction with background refresh for hot queries
Cache only aggregations computed more than 10 minutes ago
Query the database directly for each analytics request without caching

questions.explanation: Write-through caching, time-series partitioning, and background refresh optimize hot analytical query performance.

questions.question: What is the recommended architecture for a globally distributed fraud-alerting pipeline processing real-time merchant events?

options.text: Use a single central database that processes all events sequentially
Leverage a multi-region event mesh with region-local ingestion, durable streaming backbone (Kafka/Pulsar) with exactly-once semantics, and stream processor (Flink)
Batch process fraud alerts every hour for cost efficiency
Use a simple message queue in one region without any deduplication

questions.explanation: Multi-region event mesh with exactly-once semantics and Flink enables real-time risk scoring across thousands of merchants.

questions.question: How would you design a low-latency presence and typing indicator delivery system for a chat app with millions of users?

options.text: Store all presence data in a central database with periodic polling
Shard by region and user-id; fan-out presence events via regional pub/sub (Kafka/Pulsar); maintain per-user state in Redis with short TTL and periodic heartbeats
Use HTTP long-polling without any WebSocket infrastructure
Broadcast presence updates to all users globally without any regional routing

questions.explanation: Regional sharding with pub/sub backbone and Redis state management with short TTLs provides low-latency delivery for presence indicators.

questions.question: What load balancing strategy is recommended for a hybrid cloud architecture with on-premises and cloud deployments?

options.text: Route all traffic to cloud first, never use on-premises
Use layered approach: DNS geo-routing for initial routing, anycast for global IP distribution, and edge proxies for final request routing
Use a single load balancer that randomly distributes traffic
Manual routing based on time of day without automation

questions.explanation: Layered approach with DNS geo-routing, anycast, and edge proxies handles hybrid cloud latency requirements and cost considerations.

questions.question: How would you design a cross-region, event-driven ride lifecycle system for a ride-hailing platform?

options.text: Use synchronous HTTP calls between services for the entire ride lifecycle
Use per-ride ordering via Kafka partitions keyed by rideId with idempotent producers; RabbitMQ for real-time commands with at-least-once delivery; SQS as durable backup
Store all ride events in a relational database and periodically replicate
Use a simple message queue without any ordering guarantees

questions.explanation: Kafka provides ordered event streaming per ride with idempotent producers, while RabbitMQ handles real-time commands with at-least-once delivery.

questions.question: What caching strategy would you use for an ML model serving platform needing to cache both features and predictions?

options.text: Use a single cache layer for all data with the same TTL
Implement multi-tier caching with separate caches for feature vectors (short TTL, write-through) and model predictions (longer TTL, lazy loading)
Never cache predictions to ensure fresh results
Cache only model weights, never cache input features

questions.explanation: Different TTLs and loading strategies for features vs predictions optimize latency while maintaining prediction accuracy.

questions.question: How would you handle distributed transactions in an e-commerce microservices architecture for the Place Order operation?

options.text: Use a distributed two-phase commit across all services
Use the Saga pattern with either choreography (service events) or orchestration (central coordinator) for managing distributed transactions
Roll back all services to initial state if any step fails
Execute all operations in a single ACID transaction

questions.explanation: Saga pattern manages distributed transactions through sequential local transactions with compensating actions for rollback.

questions.question: What rate limiting approach implements progressive penalties for a public API with escalating violations?

options.text: Apply permanent IP blocking after first violation
Implement a multi-stage penalty system using Redis with sorted sets for tracking violation timestamps and penalty tiers with state machine escalation
Apply the same fixed penalty regardless of violation history
Send warning emails but always allow requests through

questions.explanation: Multi-stage penalties with sorted sets track violation history while state machine escalates penalties based on patterns.

title: Terraform Associate Test
description: Test your terraform-associate knowledge with 20 questions

questions.question: What is the correct CI/CD pipeline sequence for implementing OPA policy gates with Terraform when using an S3 backend?

options.text: terraform init, terraform apply, terraform show -json, opa eval
terraform init, terraform plan -out=tfplan, terraform show -json tfplan, opa eval
terraform plan, terraform apply, terraform show, opa check
terraform init, terraform validate, opa eval, terraform apply

questions.explanation: The correct sequence initializes Terraform, creates a plan file, converts it to JSON format, then evaluates it against OPA policies in CI.

questions.question: What is the recommended approach for managing network state in a dedicated repository with cross-environment Terraform deployments?

options.text: Use a single backend for all environments and copy state files manually
Use separate backends per environment; centralize VPC in network repo; read VPC outputs via terraform_remote_state data source
Export VPC outputs to JSON files and import them in application modules
Create a shared git submodule for network configuration across repos

questions.explanation: The recommended approach uses separate backends per environment and centralizes VPC in a network repo with terraform_remote_state for outputs.

questions.question: How do you detect drift for resources managed by a multi-provider Terraform module (AWS and GCP)?

options.text: Run terraform plan and look for changes in the output
Run terraform plan -detailed-exitcode to detect drift across all providers
Run terraform refresh and check for drift messages
Run provider-specific commands: aws-cli describe and gcloud compute firewall list

questions.explanation: The -detailed-exitcode flag returns non-zero when there are changes, allowing detection of drift across all providers.

questions.question: What is the correct CI pipeline to target a specific environment (prod) when using Terraform with an S3 backend having per-env state keys?

options.text: terraform init; terraform workspace select prod; terraform apply
terraform init -backend-config with environment key; terraform plan -out=tfplan; terraform show -json
terraform plan -target=envs.prod; terraform apply
terraform init -var-file=prod.tfvars; terraform apply -auto-approve

questions.explanation: Setting TARGET_ENV in CI and using backend-config for the key path targets the correct environment state.

questions.question: How do you set up a reusable QA-only preview environment in a Terraform repo with S3 backend and per-env state keys?

options.text: Create a new workspace named qa-preview and run terraform apply
Configure a QA backend alias pointing to envs/qa state, then run init with QA backend-config before planning
Clone the prod state to QA using terraform state push
Use terraform workspace new qa and set TF_WORKSPACE=qa

questions.explanation: A QA backend alias allows reusable preview that connects to the QA state path without affecting other environments.

questions.question: What is the correct configuration for multi-tenant isolation where each tenant has dedicated S3 state keys?

options.text: Use a single backend key with tenant prefixes and enable MFA
Configure per-tenant backend keys like tenants/${TENANT}/prod and use a shared DynamoDB table for locks
Create separate S3 buckets per tenant and disable state locking
Use Terraform Cloud workspaces with tenant organizations

questions.explanation: Per-tenant backend keys combined with a shared lock table provides both isolation and centralized locking.

questions.question: A drift occurred in one of 100 AWS subnets managed by for_each. What is the correct way to repair just that specific subnet?

options.text: Run terraform taint aws_subnet.all and then terraform apply
Run terraform plan to get exact address, then terraform taint with the specific subnet key
Run terraform apply -target=aws_subnet.this to recreate all subnets
Delete the state file and run terraform import for each subnet

questions.explanation: Getting the exact resource address from plan output allows targeting only the drifted subnet for repair.

questions.question: How do you add a new tenant in a monorepo managing multi-tenant VPC setup with per-tenant isolation?

options.text: Add tenant config to variables.tf and run terraform apply -target=module.vpc
Set TENANT_ID environment variable and initialize with tenant-scoped backend key, then apply
Create a new Terraform file tenants.tf and run terraform init only
Fork the repository for each new tenant

questions.explanation: Setting TENANT_ID with a tenant-scoped backend key isolates state while reusing the same module.

questions.question: What is the correct way to provision 3 private subnets across 3 AZs in a single VPC using Terraform modules?

options.text: Use count = 3 and reference count.index for availability zone
Define a map variable for subnets keyed by AZ, use for_each to create aws_subnet resources
Create three separate aws_vpc resources with different CIDRs
Use dynamic blocks with for_each on availability_zones data source

questions.explanation: A map variable with for_each allows defining each subnet with its specific AZ, CIDR, and tags.

questions.question: You are migrating from count to for_each for database environments. How do you detect and handle the prod instance drift?

options.text: Run terraform apply to automatically migrate the resource
Run terraform plan -detailed-exitcode, if returns 2 use terraform state list to find addresses, migrate using for_each syntax
Delete the old state and import the database instance
Use terraform import with the old count index

questions.explanation: Detecting drift and identifying exact addresses allows proper migration from count-based to for_each resources.

questions.question: How do you implement a dedicated QA backend in a Terraform repo using S3 with per-env state keys?

options.text: Configure a QA backend alias with key pointing to envs/qa state path
Create a separate Terraform workspace named qa
Use environment variables TF_VAR_env=qa
Add provider block with alias qa

questions.explanation: A backend alias allows reinitializing Terraform with a different state path for QA.

questions.question: What is the correct approach to pin a child module version while testing a new feature branch in your Terraform repo?

options.text: Pin module version using ref parameter in module block, run terraform init -upgrade, create feature workspace
Specify version in provider block and run terraform get
Use -target flag to apply the module
Set the module source to local path

questions.explanation: Pinning with ref parameter, running init -upgrade, and using workspaces enables controlled testing.

questions.question: How do you implement a blue/green deployment strategy in a Terraform repo with per-env state keys for prod, staging, and qa?

options.text: Use the same state for both blue and green and apply changes immediately
Create prod-preview workspace, plan against prod state, export to JSON and validate addresses start with envs/prod
Duplicate the Terraform code for blue and green environments
Run parallel terraform applies in CI

questions.explanation: A dedicated preview workspace allows validating changes before applying to production state.

questions.question: A drift was detected in a GCP firewall resource managed by a multi-cloud Terraform module. What are the exact steps to fix it?

options.text: Run terraform apply to recreate all resources
Run terraform plan -detailed-exitcode to detect drift, then import the drifted GCP firewall using its full resource address
Use gcloud commands to fix the firewall rule manually
Delete the state and run terraform refresh

questions.explanation: Detecting drift and importing the exact resource address restores the drifted GCP resource to state.

questions.question: A remote operation left a stale lock in DynamoDB. What should a beginner do to safely recover?

options.text: Delete the DynamoDB table and recreate it
Confirm lock exists, check no process running, release with terraform force-unlock, then re-run init
Run terraform apply -force to override the lock
Wait 24 hours for automatic lock expiration

questions.explanation: Confirming the lock and no active operation allows safe force-unlock to recover from stale locks.

questions.question: After an S3 regional outage corrupted your prod state, what are the exact steps for recovery?

options.text: Run terraform apply to recreate resources
Identify outage time, fetch corresponding S3 versionId, download old state locally, run terraform init -migrate-state, then validate
Create new S3 bucket and restore from backup
Recreate all resources manually from configuration

questions.explanation: Using S3 versioning to restore a pre-outage state file and migrating to it recovers corrupted state.

questions.question: How do you isolate dev and prod using workspaces with a single S3 backend in Terraform?

options.text: Create separate S3 buckets for each environment
Use workspaces and derive bucket name from terraform.workspace using a locals map
Set environment variables for each deployment
Use different Terraform configurations for each environment

questions.explanation: Using a locals map derived from workspace allows a single backend to serve multiple environments.

questions.question: What is the correct approach to test an AWS provider upgrade in a Terraform repo before applying to prod?

options.text: Apply the upgrade directly to prod
Create QA workspace and pin provider version with override file, then plan and verify
Downgrade to older provider version
Run terraform get -update to upgrade providers

questions.explanation: Creating a workspace with pinned provider version allows testing upgrades safely before production.

questions.question: What is the correct approach to provision resources in two different AWS regions using a single Terraform module?

options.text: Use count = 2 and reference element function
Configure two AWS providers with aliases and bind module instances to them, target specific provider for selective apply
Create two separate module blocks without provider aliases
Use the default provider for both regions

questions.explanation: Provider aliases allow binding resources to specific regions, and targeting enables selective apply.

questions.question: A production IAM role was accidentally deleted from state but exists in AWS. How do you recover it while using for_each over environments?

options.text: Run terraform apply to recreate automatically
Run terraform init and plan to detect drift, use terraform state show to inspect, then import with for_each address
Delete the module and recreate all roles
Use AWS CLI to recreate the IAM role

questions.explanation: Detecting drift and importing with the exact for_each address recovers the missing state resource.
